---
title: "Appendix: Theoretical Analysis of the Augmented Synthetic Historical Control Estimator (ASHC)"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=1in
    include-in-header: prelim.tex
    keep-tex: true
    cite-method: natbib
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    number-sections: true
bibliography: pl.bib
---

# Setup and Assumptions

For self-containment, let $\mathcal{T} = \{1, 2, \dots, T\}$ index time at a
monthly frequency. The pre-treatment period is
$\mathcal{T}_1 \defeq \{t \in \mathcal{T} : t \leq T_0\}$ and the post-treatment
period is $\mathcal{T}_2 \defeq \{t \in \mathcal{T} : t > T_0\}$, with
$n \defeq T - T_0$ post-treatment months. Let $m \in \mathbb{N}$ be the
evaluation-window length and define the evaluation block as the final $m$
pre-treatment months, $\mathcal{T}_{\text{eval}} \defeq \{T_0 - m + 1, \dots, T_0\} \subset \mathcal{T}_1$.

Throughout, $\widetilde{\mathbf{y}}$ and $\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}_{\text{pre}}$
denote the *kernel-smoothed* treated series, not the raw one. Writing the
smoothed pre-treatment series as $\widetilde{\mathbf y}$, SHC by \cite{SHC}
forms $N \defeq T_0 - m - n + 1$ overlapping length-$m$ segments
$\widetilde{\mathbf{y}}_{[i,\,i+m-1]}$ and stacks them into the donor matrix
$$
\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}_{\text{pre}}
\defeq
\begin{bmatrix}
\widetilde{\mathbf{y}}_{[1, m]} & \widetilde{\mathbf{y}}_{[2, m+1]} & \dots & \widetilde{\mathbf{y}}_{[N, N+m-1]}
\end{bmatrix}
\in \mathbb{R}^{m \times N}.
$$
The evaluation subvector $\widetilde{\mathbf{y}}_{\text{eval}} \in \mathbb{R}^m$
is the target pattern we reconstruct from these earlier segments. To keep the
notation light in the proofs we write $\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}} \equiv \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}_{\text{pre}}$
and $\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} \equiv \widetilde{\mathbf{y}}_{\text{eval}}$ whenever
no confusion can arise; both live in the evaluation block.

\begin{example}
If $T_0 = 60$, the segment length is $m = 12$ months, and the post-treatment
horizon is $n = 6$ months, then $N = 60 - 12 - 6 + 1 = 43$ donor segments, each
$12$ months long.
\end{example}

SHC and ASHC solve, respectively,
$$
\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}
= \underset{\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^N}{\operatorname*{argmin}}
\ \left\| \widetilde{\mathbf{y}}_{\text{eval}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}_{\text{pre}} \mathbf{w} \right\|_2^2
+ \varsigma \left\| \mathbf{C}_0^\top \mathbf{w} \right\|_2^2
\quad \text{s.t. } \mathbf{w} \geq \mathbf{0}, \ \mathbf{1}^\top \mathbf{w} = 1,
$$
$$
\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}
= \underset{\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^N}{\operatorname*{argmin}}
\ \frac{1}{2\lambda} \|\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}} \mathbf{w}\|_2^2
+ \|\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\|_2^2
+ \varsigma \|\mathbf{C}_0^\top \mathbf{w}\|_2^2
\quad \text{s.t. } \mathbf{1}^\top \mathbf{w} = 1.
$$
Here $\lambda > 0$ is the proximity (regularization) parameter, $\varsigma > 0$
is a small low-variance penalty weight, and $\mathbf{C}_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times k}$
holds the *orthonormal* eigenvectors of the donor Gram matrix
$\mathbf{G} = \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}$ whose
eigenvalues fall below a threshold $\tau > 0$ (the directions in which the
overlapping segments are nearly collinear). Two facts about $\mathbf{C}_0$ are
used repeatedly and worth stating now. Because its columns are orthonormal,
$\mathbf{C}_0^\top \mathbf{C}_0 = \mathbf{I}_k$, so the operator norm
$\|\mathbf{C}_0\| = 1$; and $\mathbf{C}_0 \mathbf{C}_0^\top$ is the orthogonal
projector onto $\operatorname{col}(\mathbf{C}_0)$, whose eigenvalues are exactly
$1$ (with multiplicity $k$) and $0$ (with multiplicity $N - k$). In particular
$\lambda_{\min}(\mathbf{C}_0 \mathbf{C}_0^\top) = 0$ whenever $k < N$.

Two differences between the programs drive everything below. First, ASHC
replaces the simplex by the *affine* constraint $\mathbf{1}^\top \mathbf{w} = 1$
(it drops $\mathbf{w} \geq \mathbf{0}$), which lets it extrapolate slightly past
the convex hull. Second, ASHC adds the proximity penalty
$\|\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\|^2$, which tethers it to the baseline SHC
solution. The proximity penalty is the source of all of ASHC's stability, as we
will see: it is what makes the objective strongly convex.

## Assumptions

\begin{assumption}[Smoothed linear representation]\label{a:lin}
There exists $\mathbf{w}^* \in \mathbb{R}^N$ with
$\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} = \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}} \mathbf{w}^* + \bm{\eta}$, where
$\bm{\eta} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the mean-zero residual of the \emph{smoothed}
evaluation block left over after the best affine match.
\end{assumption}

\begin{assumption}[Bounded smoothed residual]\label{a:noise}
$\|\bm{\eta}\| \leq \delta$ for some $\delta > 0$. Because $\bm{\eta}$ is a
residual of the smoothed series, $\delta$ shrinks as the smoothing window grows;
we make this quantitative in Section 3.
\end{assumption}

\begin{assumption}[Smoothness of the latent trend, from \citet{SHC}]\label{a:smooth}
The latent component $\ell(t)$ of the treated potential outcome is $H+1$ times
differentiable with $|\ell^{(H+1)}(t)| < \epsilon$ for all $t$ and some
$H \geq 3$.
\end{assumption}

\begin{assumption}[Operator-norm bound]\label{a:op}
$\|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\| \leq \gamma$.
\end{assumption}

\begin{assumption}[Simplex feasibility of SHC]\label{a:simplex}
$\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}} \in \Delta^{N-1} \defeq \{\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^N : \mathbf{1}^\top\mathbf{w} = 1,\ \mathbf{w} \geq \mathbf{0}\}$.
\end{assumption}

We refer to these as (A1)--(A5) in the order above, matching
Assumptions~\ref{a:lin}--\ref{a:simplex}.

A word on $\mathbf{w}^*$. With $N > m$ the system
$\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} = \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^* + \bm\eta$ is
under-determined, so $\mathbf{w}^*$ is not unique as a bare algebraic object.
We will therefore \emph{not} anchor any bound to $\mathbf{w}^*$ directly. Every
deviation below is measured against $\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$, which is a genuine
minimizer of a well-posed (strictly convex on its active face) program and is
the object we actually compute. This is the main structural change from a
naive oracle-based argument, and it is what keeps the proofs honest.


# In-Sample Analysis: Deviation From SHC

The plan for this section has two steps. First we bound how far the ASHC weight
vector can move away from the SHC weight vector --- call this deviation
$\Delta$. Then we translate that weight deviation into a deviation in the fitted
evaluation block, using the operator norm of the donor matrix. The tools are the
first-order (KKT) conditions, the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality, and Weyl's
eigenvalue inequality.

\begin{lemma}[Deviation from SHC]\label{lem:delta}
Let $\Delta \defeq \mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}} - \mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$ and
$\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} \defeq \|\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\|$.
Write $\rho \defeq \|\mathbf{C}_0^\top \mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\|$, the size of the
SHC weights along the penalized low-variance directions. Then
\[
\|\Delta\| \;\leq\; \frac{\gamma}{2\lambda}\,\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} \;+\; \varsigma\,\rho,
\qquad \text{and since } \rho \leq 1, \qquad
\|\Delta\| \;\leq\; \frac{\gamma}{2\lambda}\,\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} + \varsigma.
\]
In particular, when the low-variance penalty is switched off
($\varsigma = 0$), $\|\Delta\| \leq \gamma\,\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}/(2\lambda)$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
\textbf{Step 1: the objective and its gradient.} Recall the ASHC objective,
\begin{equation}\label{eq:J}
J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2\lambda} \|\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}} \mathbf{w}\|^2
+ \|\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\|^2
+ \varsigma \|\mathbf{C}_0^\top \mathbf{w}\|^2,
\end{equation}
minimized subject to $\mathbf{1}^\top \mathbf{w} = 1$. We attach a multiplier
$\mu$ to the constraint and form the Lagrangian
$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \mu) = J(\mathbf{w}) + \mu(\mathbf{1}^\top \mathbf{w} - 1)$,
then differentiate term by term.

For the fit term, set $r(\mathbf{w}) = \widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}} \mathbf{w}$.
The gradient of $\tfrac{1}{2\lambda}\|r\|^2$ is, by the chain rule,
$\tfrac{1}{2\lambda}\,(\nabla_r \|r\|^2)^\top \nabla_{\mathbf w} r
= \tfrac{1}{2\lambda}(2r)^\top(-\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}})
= -\tfrac{1}{\lambda}\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top r
= \tfrac{1}{\lambda}\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top(\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w} - \widetilde{\mathbf{y}})$.
The proximity term contributes $2(\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}})$; the
low-variance term, written $\varsigma\,\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{C}_0 \mathbf{C}_0^\top \mathbf{w}$,
contributes $2\varsigma \mathbf{C}_0\mathbf{C}_0^\top \mathbf{w}$; and the
constraint contributes $\mu\mathbf{1}$. Setting the total to zero gives the
first-order condition
\begin{equation}\label{eq:foc}
\frac{1}{\lambda} \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top (\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}} \mathbf{w} - \widetilde{\mathbf{y}})
+ 2 (\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}})
+ 2 \varsigma \mathbf{C}_0 \mathbf{C}_0^\top \mathbf{w}
+ \mu \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{0}.
\end{equation}

\textbf{Step 2: collect into a matrix.} Define the (symmetric) matrix
\begin{equation}\label{eq:M}
\mathbf{M} \defeq \frac{1}{\lambda} \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}
+ 2 \mathbf{I} + 2 \varsigma \mathbf{C}_0 \mathbf{C}_0^\top,
\end{equation}
which is exactly the Hessian of $J$. Gathering the $\mathbf{w}$ terms in
\eqref{eq:foc}, the ASHC solution satisfies
\begin{equation}\label{eq:Mashc}
\mathbf{M}\,\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} + 2\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}} - \mu\mathbf{1}.
\end{equation}
Now apply the *same* matrix $\mathbf{M}$ to $\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$ --- this is
just the identity $\mathbf{M}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}} = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$,
written out:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:Mshc}
\mathbf{M}\,\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}} + 2\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}} + 2\varsigma\mathbf{C}_0\mathbf{C}_0^\top\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}.
\end{equation}
Subtract \eqref{eq:Mshc} from \eqref{eq:Mashc}. The $2\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$
terms cancel, but --- and this is the step that must be done carefully --- the
$2\varsigma\mathbf{C}_0\mathbf{C}_0^\top\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$ term does
\emph{not} cancel, because the ASHC side has no matching $\varsigma$ term at
$\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$. Writing $r = \widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$,
\begin{equation}\label{eq:MDelta}
\mathbf{M}\Delta
= \frac{1}{\lambda}\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top r - \mu\mathbf{1} - 2\varsigma\,\mathbf{C}_0\mathbf{C}_0^\top\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}.
\end{equation}

\textbf{Step 3: the energy identity.} Both $\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}$ and
$\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$ satisfy $\mathbf{1}^\top\mathbf{w} = 1$, so their
difference is orthogonal to $\mathbf{1}$: $\mathbf{1}^\top\Delta = 0$. Take the
inner product of \eqref{eq:MDelta} with $\Delta$; the multiplier term drops out:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:energy}
\Delta^\top \mathbf{M}\Delta = \frac{1}{\lambda}\Delta^\top\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top r - 2\varsigma\,\Delta^\top\mathbf{C}_0\mathbf{C}_0^\top\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}.
\end{equation}

\textbf{Step 4: bound the right-hand side.} Two inner products to control. For
the first, Cauchy--Schwarz and the operator-norm bound (A4) give
$|\Delta^\top\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top r| \leq \|\Delta\|\,\|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top r\| \leq \|\Delta\|\,\|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\|\,\|r\| \leq \gamma\,\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}\,\|\Delta\|$.
For the second, since $\mathbf{C}_0$ has orthonormal columns
($\|\mathbf{C}_0^\top \mathbf v\| \le \|\mathbf v\|$),
$|\Delta^\top\mathbf{C}_0\mathbf{C}_0^\top\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}|
= |(\mathbf{C}_0^\top\Delta)^\top(\mathbf{C}_0^\top\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}})|
\leq \|\mathbf{C}_0^\top\Delta\|\,\|\mathbf{C}_0^\top\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\|
\leq \|\Delta\|\,\rho$, where $\rho = \|\mathbf{C}_0^\top\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\|$.
Therefore
\begin{equation}\label{eq:rhsbound}
\Delta^\top\mathbf{M}\Delta \;\leq\; \frac{\gamma}{\lambda}\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}\|\Delta\| + 2\varsigma\rho\|\Delta\|.
\end{equation}

\textbf{Step 5: bound the left-hand side via Weyl.} The matrix $\mathbf{M}$ in
\eqref{eq:M} is a sum of three symmetric positive-\emph{semi}definite pieces.
Weyl's inequality, $\lambda_{\min}(\mathbf A + \mathbf B) \geq \lambda_{\min}(\mathbf A) + \lambda_{\min}(\mathbf B)$,
applied twice gives
\[
\lambda_{\min}(\mathbf{M})
\geq \lambda_{\min}\!\Big(\tfrac1\lambda\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\Big)
+ \lambda_{\min}(2\mathbf{I})
+ \lambda_{\min}(2\varsigma\mathbf{C}_0\mathbf{C}_0^\top)
= 0 + 2 + 0 = 2.
\]
The first term is $0$ because $\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}^\top\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}$
has rank at most $m < N$, hence a zero eigenvalue; the third is $0$ because
$\mathbf{C}_0\mathbf{C}_0^\top$ is a rank-$k$ projector, whose smallest
eigenvalue is $0$. So the $\varsigma$-penalty does \emph{not} raise the
curvature floor --- all of the strong convexity comes from the proximity term's
$2\mathbf{I}$. Consequently $\mathbf{M} \succeq 2\mathbf{I} \succ 0$, and
\begin{equation}\label{eq:lhsbound}
\Delta^\top\mathbf{M}\Delta \geq 2\|\Delta\|^2.
\end{equation}

\textbf{Step 6: combine.} Putting \eqref{eq:lhsbound} and \eqref{eq:rhsbound}
together and assuming $\|\Delta\| > 0$ (otherwise the claim is trivial), divide
through by $\|\Delta\|$:
\[
2\|\Delta\| \;\leq\; \frac{\gamma}{\lambda}\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} + 2\varsigma\rho
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\|\Delta\| \;\leq\; \frac{\gamma}{2\lambda}\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} + \varsigma\rho.
\]
Finally $\rho = \|\mathbf{C}_0^\top\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\| \leq \|\mathbf{C}_0^\top\|\,\|\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\| = \|\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\| \leq \|\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\|_1 = 1$,
using simplex feasibility (A5). This gives the stated bounds.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
The shape of the bound is the point. The deviation is governed by
$\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}$ --- the SHC residual --- scaled by $\gamma/(2\lambda)$.
The proximity strength $\lambda$ sits in the denominator, so a tighter leash
(small $\lambda$) forces ASHC to stay near SHC, while $\gamma$ in the numerator
says a more amplifying donor matrix permits larger swings. The penalty term
contributes only the additive $\varsigma\rho$, and even that is benign: SHC's
own objective penalizes $\|\mathbf{C}_0^\top\mathbf w\|^2$, so $\rho$ is small by
construction, and $\varsigma$ is a tiny numerical safeguard (in practice
$\varsigma = 10^{-6}$). The honest reading is therefore: ASHC moves away from
SHC only to the extent that SHC fit poorly ($\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}$ large), and
the proximity penalty --- not the low-variance penalty --- is what guarantees
the movement is controlled. This corrects a tempting but false intuition that
the $\varsigma$ penalty appears in the denominator: it does not, because
$\mathbf{C}_0\mathbf{C}_0^\top$ is rank-deficient and contributes nothing to
$\lambda_{\min}(\mathbf{M})$.
\end{remark}

We now translate a weight deviation into a fitted-outcome deviation.

\begin{lemma}[Operator-norm propagation]\label{lem:gamma}
Under (A4), $\|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\Delta\| \leq \gamma\|\Delta\|$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The operator norm is defined as
$\|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\| = \sup_{\mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf 0}\|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{v}\|/\|\mathbf{v}\|$,
the largest factor by which $\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}$ can stretch a vector. Taking
$\mathbf v = \Delta$ gives
$\|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\Delta\| \leq \|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\|\,\|\Delta\|$,
and (A4) bounds $\|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\| \leq \gamma$. In words: even though
ASHC's weights differ from SHC's, the change they induce in the reconstructed
evaluation block cannot exceed the weight change amplified by $\gamma$.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[In-sample fit of ASHC]\label{prop:insample}
Under (A4)--(A5), the ASHC evaluation-block fit satisfies
\[
\big\|\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}\big\|
\;\leq\; \epsilon_{\text{SHC}}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma^2}{2\lambda}\right) + \gamma\,\varsigma\rho.
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Add and subtract $\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$ and use the
triangle inequality:
\[
\big\|\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}\big\|
= \big\|(\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}) - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\Delta\big\|
\leq \epsilon_{\text{SHC}} + \|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\Delta\|.
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma}, $\|\widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\Delta\| \leq \gamma\|\Delta\|$,
and by Lemma~\ref{lem:delta}, $\|\Delta\| \leq \gamma\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}/(2\lambda) + \varsigma\rho$.
Substituting,
\[
\big\|\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}\big\|
\leq \epsilon_{\text{SHC}} + \gamma\Big(\tfrac{\gamma}{2\lambda}\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} + \varsigma\rho\Big)
= \epsilon_{\text{SHC}}\Big(1 + \tfrac{\gamma^2}{2\lambda}\Big) + \gamma\varsigma\rho. \qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
Note what this bound does and does \emph{not} say. It is a purely in-sample
statement: it controls the fit on the evaluation block, where ASHC's own
objective explicitly contains the matching term, so ASHC can do no worse than a
bounded multiple of the SHC fit. The smoothness bias and noise of the
post-treatment forecast do \emph{not} belong here --- they enter only when we
extrapolate past $T_0$, which is the subject of the next section. Keeping the
in-sample and out-of-sample errors separate is the cleanest way to see where
each source of error comes from.
\end{remark}


# Out-of-Sample Analysis: Post-Treatment Prediction and the ATT

Past $T_0$ we no longer observe the matching target, so two new error sources
appear: the Taylor remainder from extrapolating the smooth latent trend, and the
post-treatment noise. We import the first from \cite{SHC} and bound everything
against $\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$ --- never against the unidentified
$\mathbf{w}^*$.

\begin{lemma}[Latent extrapolation bias, from \citet{SHC}]\label{lem:smooth}
Under (A3), for any horizon $k > 0$,
\[
\big|\ell(T_0 + k) - \ell^{\text{SHC}}(T_0 + k)\big|
\;\leq\; b_\epsilon(H,k) \;\defeq\; \frac{2\epsilon\,|k|^{H+1}}{(H+1)!}.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
This is Proposition 1 of \cite{SHC}. The intuition: SHC reproduces the first
$H$ terms of the Taylor expansion of $\ell$ around $T_0$ from the historical
segments, so the only discrepancy is the $(H+1)$-st order remainder, which (A3)
bounds by $\epsilon|k|^{H+1}/(H+1)!$ up to the constant $2$ coming from
combining the treated and reconstructed remainders.
\end{proof}

Stacking Lemma~\ref{lem:smooth} over the post-treatment block gives the SHC
counterfactual error directly.

\begin{lemma}[SHC post-treatment error]\label{lem:shc_post}
Under (A2)--(A3), the SHC counterfactual error over $\mathcal{T}_2$ satisfies
\[
\big\|\mathbf{y}_{\text{post}} - \mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\big\|
\;\leq\; b_{\epsilon,\text{post}}(H) + \delta_{\text{post}},
\]
where $b_{\epsilon,\text{post}}(H) \defeq \big(\sum_{k=1}^{n} b_\epsilon(H,k)^2\big)^{1/2}$
collects the per-horizon extrapolation biases of Lemma~\ref{lem:smooth}, and
$\delta_{\text{post}}$ bounds the post-treatment noise $\bm\eta_{\text{post}}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Write $\mathbf{y}_{\text{post}} = \mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}} + \mathbf{b}_{\text{post}} + \bm\eta_{\text{post}}$,
where $\mathbf{b}_{\text{post}}$ is the vector of latent extrapolation errors
($k = 1, \dots, n$) and $\bm\eta_{\text{post}}$ is the post-treatment noise. The
triangle inequality gives
$\|\mathbf{y}_{\text{post}} - \mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\| \leq \|\mathbf{b}_{\text{post}}\| + \|\bm\eta_{\text{post}}\|$.
By Lemma~\ref{lem:smooth}, the $k$-th entry of $\mathbf{b}_{\text{post}}$ is at
most $b_\epsilon(H,k)$, so $\|\mathbf{b}_{\text{post}}\| \leq b_{\epsilon,\text{post}}(H)$;
by (A2), $\|\bm\eta_{\text{post}}\| \leq \delta_{\text{post}}$.
\end{proof}

\begin{assumption}[Post-treatment operator norm]\label{a:op_post}
There exists $\gamma_{\text{post}} > 0$ with $\|\mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\| \leq \gamma_{\text{post}}$.
\end{assumption}

\begin{proposition}[Post-treatment prediction and ATT error]\label{prop:att}
Under (A1)--(A5) and Assumption~\ref{a:op_post}, the ASHC counterfactual error
satisfies
\[
\big\|\mathbf{y}_{\text{post}} - \mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}\big\|
\;\leq\;
b_{\epsilon,\text{post}}(H) + \delta_{\text{post}}
+ \gamma_{\text{post}}\Big(\frac{\gamma}{2\lambda}\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} + \varsigma\rho\Big),
\]
and consequently the ATT estimate obeys
\[
\big|\widehat{\mathrm{ATT}} - \mathrm{ATT}\big|
\;\leq\;
\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\left[
b_{\epsilon,\text{post}}(H) + \delta_{\text{post}}
+ \gamma_{\text{post}}\Big(\frac{\gamma}{2\lambda}\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} + \varsigma\rho\Big)
\right].
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
\textbf{Prediction error.} Decompose against $\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$ (not the
oracle), then use the triangle inequality:
\[
\big\|\mathbf{y}_{\text{post}} - \mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}\big\|
= \big\|(\mathbf{y}_{\text{post}} - \mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}) - \mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\Delta\big\|
\leq \underbrace{\big\|\mathbf{y}_{\text{post}} - \mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\big\|}_{\text{SHC counterfactual error}}
+ \underbrace{\big\|\mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\Delta\big\|}_{\text{ASHC's extra move}}.
\]
The first term is bounded by $b_{\epsilon,\text{post}}(H) + \delta_{\text{post}}$
(Lemma~\ref{lem:shc_post}). For the second, Assumption~\ref{a:op_post} gives
$\|\mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\Delta\| \leq \gamma_{\text{post}}\|\Delta\|$, and
Lemma~\ref{lem:delta} bounds $\|\Delta\| \leq \gamma\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}/(2\lambda) + \varsigma\rho$.
Adding the two pieces yields the prediction bound. Note that everything is
anchored at $\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}$, so no claim about the non-unique
$\mathbf{w}^*$ is needed.

\textbf{ATT error.} The estimated and true ATT differ only through the
counterfactual:
$\widehat{\mathrm{ATT}} - \mathrm{ATT} = \tfrac1n \sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}_2}\big(y_t(0) - \widehat{y}_t(0)\big)$.
Taking absolute values and applying the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality
$\sum_{t}|a_t| \leq \sqrt{n}\,\|\mathbf a\|$,
\[
\big|\widehat{\mathrm{ATT}} - \mathrm{ATT}\big|
\leq \frac1n \sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}_2}\big|y_t(0) - \widehat{y}_t(0)\big|
\leq \frac{1}{\sqrt n}\big\|\mathbf{y}_{\text{post}} - \mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}\big\|.
\]
Substituting the prediction bound finishes the proof.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
The $1/\sqrt{n}$ in the ATT bound is doing real work. The prediction error is an
$n$-vector whose Euclidean norm grows like $\sqrt n$ when the per-period errors
are $O(1)$; averaging into a scalar ATT and bounding by Cauchy--Schwarz puts
back exactly one factor of $\sqrt n$, leaving a per-period quantity. This is the
right scale on which to ask for consistency, and it is why the asymptotics below
are stated for the per-period (root-mean-square) error rather than the raw
Euclidean norm.
\end{remark}


# Asymptotic Results

Two warnings shape this section. First, $\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} = \|\widetilde{\mathbf{y}}_{\text{eval}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}_{\text{pre}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{SHC}}\|$
is the Euclidean norm of an $m$-vector; if we let $m \to \infty$ with $O(1)$
per-period residuals, this norm grows like $\sqrt m$ and cannot converge to
zero. The quantity that can converge is the per-period root-mean-square error
\[
\bar\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} \defeq \frac{1}{\sqrt m}\,\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}.
\]
Second, $\widetilde{\mathbf y}$ is the \emph{smoothed} series, so the residual
$\bm\eta$ in (A1)--(A2) is a smoothing residual, not raw noise; its per-period
size is reduced by the kernel and goes to zero, which is what makes consistency
possible. We state results for $\bar\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}$ and for the ATT.

\begin{theorem}[Consistency]\label{thm:consistency}
Under (A1)--(A5), suppose the kernel bandwidth $h_m \to 0$ with $m h_m \to \infty$
as $m \to \infty$, and let $H \to \infty$. Then the per-period ASHC fit error
satisfies
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt m}\big\|\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}\big\| \xrightarrow{p} 0.
\]
If in addition $\mathbf{Y}_{\text{post}}$ obeys Assumption~\ref{a:op_post} with
$\gamma_{\text{post}} = O(\sqrt n)$ and the post-treatment noise has per-period
variance vanishing under smoothing, then
$\big|\widehat{\mathrm{ATT}} - \mathrm{ATT}\big| \xrightarrow{p} 0$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
From Proposition~\ref{prop:insample}, dividing by $\sqrt m$,
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt m}\big\|\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}\big\|
\leq \bar\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}\Big(1 + \frac{\gamma^2}{2\lambda}\Big) + \frac{\gamma\varsigma\rho}{\sqrt m}.
\]
The inflation factor $1 + \gamma^2/(2\lambda)$ is a fixed constant, and
$\gamma\varsigma\rho/\sqrt m \to 0$ since $\rho \le 1$ and $\varsigma$ is fixed.
It remains to show $\bar\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} \xrightarrow{p} 0$. The smoothed
matching residual decomposes into a smoothing bias and a stochastic part. By the
smoothness assumption (A3), a kernel smoother that reproduces $H$ Taylor terms
has per-period bias $O(h_m^{H+1})$; the stochastic part of a kernel average over
$\asymp m h_m$ points is $O_p\big((m h_m)^{-1/2}\big)$. Hence
\[
\bar\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} = O_p\big(h_m^{H+1} + (m h_m)^{-1/2}\big) \xrightarrow{p} 0
\]
under $h_m \to 0$, $m h_m \to \infty$. This proves the first claim. For the
second, Proposition~\ref{prop:att} bounds the ATT error by
$n^{-1/2}\big[b_{\epsilon,\text{post}}(H) + \delta_{\text{post}} + \gamma_{\text{post}}(\tfrac{\gamma}{2\lambda}\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} + \varsigma\rho)\big]$.
As $H \to \infty$, $b_\epsilon(H,k) = 2\epsilon|k|^{H+1}/(H+1)! \to 0$ for each
fixed $k$, so $n^{-1/2} b_{\epsilon,\text{post}}(H) \to 0$; the smoothed
post-noise term $n^{-1/2}\delta_{\text{post}} \to 0$ by assumption; and
$n^{-1/2}\gamma_{\text{post}}\cdot \tfrac{\gamma}{2\lambda}\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}
= O(1)\cdot \tfrac{\gamma}{2\lambda}\cdot \tfrac{\sqrt m}{\sqrt n}\bar\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} \xrightarrow{p} 0$.
The remaining $n^{-1/2}\gamma_{\text{post}}\varsigma\rho = O(\varsigma) \to 0$ as
$\varsigma \to 0$ (or is negligible at the fixed small $\varsigma$ used in
practice). All terms vanish.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Convergence rate]\label{cor:rate}
Under the conditions of Theorem~\ref{thm:consistency}, the optimal bandwidth
balancing the two error sources is $h_m \asymp m^{-1/(2H+3)}$, at which
\[
\bar\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} = O_p\big(m^{-(H+1)/(2H+3)}\big),
\qquad\text{and hence}\qquad
\frac{1}{\sqrt m}\big\|\widetilde{\mathbf{y}} - \widetilde{\mathbf{Y}}\mathbf{w}^{\text{ASHC}}\big\| = O_p\big(m^{-(H+1)/(2H+3)}\big).
\]
As $H \to \infty$, the exponent $(H+1)/(2H+3) \to \tfrac12$, so the rate
approaches the parametric $O_p(m^{-1/2})$.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
From the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:consistency},
$\bar\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} = O_p(h_m^{H+1} + (m h_m)^{-1/2})$. Equating the two
orders, $h_m^{H+1} \asymp (m h_m)^{-1/2}$, gives $h_m^{2H+3} \asymp m^{-1}$, i.e.
$h_m \asymp m^{-1/(2H+3)}$. At this bandwidth each piece is
$h_m^{H+1} \asymp m^{-(H+1)/(2H+3)}$, so
$\bar\epsilon_{\text{SHC}} = O_p(m^{-(H+1)/(2H+3)})$. The ASHC fit inflates this
only by the constant $1 + \gamma^2/(2\lambda)$ (Proposition~\ref{prop:insample}),
leaving the rate unchanged. Finally
$(H+1)/(2H+3) = \tfrac12 \cdot \tfrac{2H+2}{2H+3} \to \tfrac12$ as $H \to \infty$.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
The exponent deserves a sentence, because it is easy to get off by one. The
latent trend is $(H+1)$-times differentiable, so a smoother that fits $H$ Taylor
terms has bias of order $h^{H+1}$, not $h^{H}$; balancing $h^{H+1}$ against the
$(mh)^{-1/2}$ variance is what produces the denominator $2H+3$ (rather than
$2H+1$) and the numerator $H+1$ (rather than $H$). Note also that this rate
governs the \emph{estimation} error $\bar\epsilon_{\text{SHC}}$ through the
kernel bandwidth; it is distinct from the \emph{extrapolation} bias
$b_\epsilon(H,k)$ of Lemma~\ref{lem:smooth}, which is fixed for a fixed horizon
$k$ and vanishes only as $H \to \infty$. Conflating the two --- treating the
extrapolation bias as if it depended on the bandwidth --- is the one slip to
avoid here.
\end{remark}
